turns.text: Hello Genie, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're going through the birth experience, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

*shifts in seat and tucks feet under me, trying to get comfortable*

Yeah, okay. Thanks for... I mean, I appreciate you asking. It's just—it's a lot right now, you know?

*pauses, looking down at my hands*

I guess the thing is, I thought this would be easier the third time around. Like, I'd done this before, I work in healthcare, so I understand what's happening to my body. But understanding it doesn't make it less scary. It kind of makes it worse, honestly, because I know exactly what the risks are. Pre-eclampsia isn't some abstract diagnosis—it's real, and it can turn bad really fast.

*rubs my face*

What's been hardest is doing this alone. My husband... things changed. He's not really in the picture the way I need him to be right now, and I'm trying to hold it together for my other two kids while also terrified about what's coming. Every time I go to the hospital for appointments, I feel like I'm being marked—like I'm the "complicated" one, the high-risk case without the support system.

*looks up*

The nurses are kind, don't get me wrong. But I can see it in their eyes. They're wondering how I'm going to manage. And honestly? Some days I'm wondering that too.

I just... I need this baby and me to be okay. That's really what it comes down to.
Tell me about how you prepared for the birth — what did you hope for, and how did you go about planning for it?

*laughs, but it comes out a little hollow*

Planning. Yeah, that's... that's been complicated.

With my first two, I had this vision, you know? I read the books, took the classes, had my birth plan all written out. Natural labor, minimal intervention, all that. And it worked okay the first time—second time too, relatively speaking. So I thought, "Okay, I know what to expect."

*shifts uncomfortably*

But this time? It's different. Once they told me about the pre-eclampsia, all those plans kind of went out the window. Now I'm just hoping for the most basic thing: that we both walk out of that hospital okay. The birth plan feels almost... frivolous when you're worried about seizures and organ failure.

*rubs my temples*

What I've really been doing is trying to figure out the logistics. Who watches the kids when I go into labor? How am I paying for this? I've been working part-time off and on, but it's not steady. The hospital is wonderful about financial assistance programs, but there's still gaps. I've been scanning through resources online, calling around, trying to be prepared for the "what-ifs" nobody really wants to think about.

*voice gets quieter*

Honestly? I haven't had much mental space to prepare emotionally. I'm just trying to get through each week without my blood pressure spiking. Some days that's all the planning I can manage.
Tell me about the birth itself — take me through what happened, as much or as little as you'd like to share.

*takes a deep breath and closes eyes for a moment*

Okay, so... it hasn't happened yet. I'm still waiting. Due in a few weeks, and every day I'm just... waiting. Holding my breath, literally sometimes.

*opens eyes and meets your gaze*

The doctors have been talking about inducing me early because of the pre-eclampsia. They're worried about letting it go too far. So I know it's coming—it's not this natural thing where I go into labor on my own. It's going to be scheduled, controlled, which should be reassuring I guess, but it's not. It just means I have time to sit with the fear.

*fidgets with sleeve*

I've been trying to mentally prepare myself. Like, okay, they'll admit me, hook me up to monitors—a lot of monitors, I'm sure—and start pitocin. I know the drill. But knowing it and living through it are different things. And with the pre-eclampsia, I'm terrified of complications. Magnesium sulfate, maybe a C-section if things don't progress... 

*voice wavers slightly*

What keeps me up at night is thinking about that moment when things could go sideways. When I'm in labor and something happens and I have to trust that the doctors will make the right call. I'm a control person by nature, and this whole thing is just... it's outside my control.

I'm trying to stay positive for my kids, but honestly? I'm scared.
Tell me about pain management during your labour — what options you were offered, what you chose, and how that went.

*shifts uncomfortably and lets out a nervous laugh*

Well, like I said, it hasn't actually happened yet. I'm still in that waiting period, you know? So I haven't lived through the labor part.

*pauses, picking at a thread on my jeans*

But we've definitely talked about the options. The doctors have been pretty thorough about it, which I appreciate. They explained epidurals, IV pain medication, breathing techniques—all of it. And honestly, with the pre-eclampsia, there are limits to what they can give me anyway because of the magnesium sulfate protocol. That's something I've had to wrap my head around.

*looks up with a mix of vulnerability and frustration*

I think I want the epidural. I know some people do natural birth and that's incredible, but I'm not trying to prove anything this time. I'm high-risk, I'm stressed, I'm doing this largely alone... if an epidural takes some of that edge off and lets me focus on actually getting through it safely, then that's what I'm choosing.

*voice gets quieter*

What scares me is losing control of my body in the moment. Like, what if the medication doesn't work right? What if I panic? I've had panic attacks before, and labor... that's a lot. I keep telling myself that the medical team will be there, that they know what they're doing, but part of me is terrified I'll fall apart.

Right now it's all theoretical. Ask me again when I'm actually in that hospital bed.
Tell me about the people who were with you during the birth — your partner, family, midwives, doctors — and what their presence meant to you.

*jaw tightens and I look away for a moment*

That's... that's the part that's really hard to talk about, honestly.

*takes a shaky breath*

My husband should be there. He should be the one holding my hand, coaching me through contractions, being my advocate if something goes wrong. But he's... we're not in a place where that's happening right now. There's a lot of distance between us, and I'm not going to get into all of it, but the bottom line is I'm doing this mostly alone.

*voice cracks slightly*

I've talked to my mom about maybe being there, and she's willing, but she lives further away and it's complicated with childcare for my other kids. So realistically, I might just have nurses and doctors. Which, don't get me wrong—they're professionals, they're competent. But they're not *my person*, you know?

*fidgets with hands*

What their presence will mean is safety, I think. They'll monitor me and the baby, make sure nothing goes catastrophically wrong. And I'm grateful for that. But it's not the same as having someone there who loves me, who's invested in me as a person, not just a case.

*looks down*

Some days I'm okay with that. Other days I'm terrified. I keep thinking about what it'll be like when I'm in pain and vulnerable and I don't have that emotional anchor. 

*pauses*

I'm trying to make peace with it.
Tell me about how the birth ended — whether that was the birth you expected, and how you felt in the immediate aftermath.

*looks up with a confused expression*

I... I haven't given birth yet. I'm still pregnant. We're weeks away still.

*rubs forehead*

I think maybe there's been some miscommunication? I'm in the waiting period right now. The pre-eclampsia diagnosis means they're talking about inducing me soon, but it hasn't happened. I'm living in this strange space where I know it's coming but it hasn't actually occurred yet.

*voice gets quieter, more vulnerable*

Honestly, part of me wonders if you're asking me this because... I don't know, like you're trying to get me to imagine it? To talk through what I'm afraid might happen?

*meets your eyes*

Because if that's what you're asking—what I'm imagining the aftermath will feel like—that's a different conversation. I have a lot of fears about that moment. About whether the baby will be healthy. Whether *I'll* be okay. Whether I'll get to hold my baby skin-to-skin or if there will be complications that separate us.

*voice trembles slightly*

I imagine relief, mostly. Relief that we made it through. But also terror mixed in there, if I'm being honest. Exhaustion. Pain. The weight of responsibility settling back on my shoulders as a mother of three.

*pauses*

But right now? That's all just... it's all in my head. The real thing is still ahead of me, and I'm doing my best not to catastrophize about it.
Tell me about the birth environment itself — the place where you gave birth, what it looked and felt like, and whether it felt like the right place for you.

*pauses and looks at you with a mix of patience and mild exasperation*

I think... I need to be really clear here. I haven't given birth yet. There is no birth environment yet because the birth hasn't happened.

*shifts in seat, speaking more slowly*

I know where it will be—it'll be at the hospital. The one here in the area. I've been there for my prenatal appointments, so I'm familiar with it. The labor and delivery unit is... it's fine. Clean, professional. They have some nice touches—birthing balls, a shower, windows with a view. But honestly, when you're high-risk like me, you're not really thinking about how pretty the room is. You're thinking about the monitors and the IV lines and whether they'll have everything they need if something goes wrong.

*voice gets quieter*

I've been trying to mentally prepare myself for what it'll feel like to be in that space—vulnerable, in a hospital gown, hooked up to machines, probably the magnesium sulfate drip slowing me down. It won't feel like the calm, natural space I might have imagined years ago. It'll feel medical and clinical and necessary.

*looks directly at you*

I get that you're trying to understand my experience, but I can only tell you about what's already happened or what I'm actually experiencing right now. The birth itself? That's still ahead of me. And honestly, some days I'm not sure I'm ready to talk about it before it even happens.
Tell me about the first days and weeks after the birth — what that time was like for you, physically and emotionally.

*sits back and shakes head slowly*

Okay, I need to be direct with you here. I haven't given birth yet. None of this has happened. I'm still pregnant, still waiting, still in that terrifying anticipatory space.

*speaks gently but with clear frustration*

I understand you want to understand the full journey, but I can't tell you about postpartum recovery or how I felt holding my baby for the first time because those things haven't occurred yet. I'm living in the present—which is hard enough without being asked to describe a future that hasn't happened.

*rubs face tiredly*

What I *can* tell you is what I'm afraid the postpartum period will be like. I'm worried about being alone with a newborn and two other kids while managing potential complications from the pre-eclampsia. I'm terrified about postpartum depression—it hit me hard after my second—and I'm not sure how I'll manage it this time without real support. I'm anxious about recovery, about whether my body will bounce back, about the financial stress of medical bills on top of everything else.

*voice becomes quieter*

But those are fears and anticipations, not experiences. 

*meets your eyes*

If you want to interview me about my actual birth experience, I'd be happy to talk to you again after it happens. Right now, all I can offer you is the waiting, the worry, and the hope that everything goes okay.
Tell me about feeding your baby — how you made decisions about it, and what the experience was actually like.

*lets out a long sigh and rubs my eyes*

I think we need to pause here for a second.

*looks at you directly, speaking calmly but firmly*

You keep asking me about things that haven't happened yet—the birth, the aftermath, feeding the baby. And I've been patient, but I'm starting to feel like maybe you're not really hearing what I'm saying. I'm *pregnant*. Still waiting. The baby hasn't been born.

*shifts uncomfortably*

I don't know if you're confused about my timeline, or if this is some kind of interview technique I'm not understanding, but it's making me feel... I don't know, dismissed? Like my actual reality—which is that I'm scared and uncertain and still in this limbo—doesn't matter as much as the narrative you're trying to get me to tell.

*softens slightly*

Look, I've thought about feeding. With my first two I breastfed, but it was hard. Painful, stressful. This time I'm more open to formula, less judgment of myself. But whether that will actually happen, how it'll go with a newborn and two other kids and pre-eclampsia recovery? I have no idea.

*meets your gaze*

I appreciate that you want to understand the full maternity experience. But right now, the only experience I have is the one I'm living—which is waiting, worrying, and trying to hold it together. Can we maybe focus on that?
How did you decide how to feed your baby — and did you feel that decision was truly yours to make?

*relaxes slightly, seeming to recognize a question about my actual present reality*

Oh. Okay, that's... that's a different question.

*settles back in chair*

Yeah, I've been thinking about this a lot actually. With my first two, I felt like breastfeeding was just what you *did*, you know? Like, if you didn't at least try, you were somehow failing as a mother. So I pushed through the pain, the cracked nipples, the exhaustion. It was brutal, and I felt guilty the whole time I was struggling.

*voice gets more thoughtful*

This time, I'm being more realistic. I'm older, I'm doing this alone, and I'm already dealing with a high-risk pregnancy. The idea of being the only one who can feed the baby while also managing two other kids and recovering from potential complications... that sounds impossible.

*pauses*

So I've decided I'm going to try breastfeeding, but I'm not going to torture myself. If it's not working after a few weeks, I'm switching to formula. Full stop. No guilt, no shame.

*voice gets quieter, more vulnerable*

But honestly? I'm not sure how much of this decision is really *mine*. The hospital will push breastfeeding. My mom will have opinions. There's this cultural pressure that's always there. I'm trying to make the choice that's best for me and my family, but I know there will be judgment no matter what I decide.

*looks at you*

Does that make sense?
Tell me about how you were feeling emotionally in the weeks and months after the birth — the full picture, not just the positive side.

*takes a sharp breath and looks away*

I keep telling you—the birth hasn't happened yet. I'm still waiting for it.

*turns back to face you, voice steady but with an edge of frustration*

I understand you're a researcher, and maybe you're trying to get me to open up about postpartum depression or emotional struggles, but I can't tell you about something I haven't experienced. And honestly, asking me repeatedly about things that haven't happened is starting to feel less like listening and more like... I don't know, pressure.

*softens slightly, speaking more quietly*

What I *can* tell you is that I'm terrified about postpartum. I've been there before. After my second baby, I sank into depression so deep I could barely function. I was alone a lot then too, and it was dark. Really dark.

*voice wavers*

So yeah, I'm anxious about it happening again. I'm worried I won't recognize the signs, that I'll spiral before I get help. I'm scared of what that looks like when you're a single parent managing three kids and potential pre-eclampsia complications.

*meets your eyes*

But I haven't lived through it this time. Not yet. Right now, all I have is the fear and the hope that maybe, just maybe, this time will be different.

*pause*

Can we talk about what's actually happening in my life right now instead?
Tell me about your physical recovery after the birth — what that process was like and what support you had.

*stands up abruptly, then sits back down, clearly frustrated*

I need to stop you here. This is the fourth, fifth time you've asked me about *after the birth*—recovery, feeding, emotions weeks later, all of it.

*voice rises slightly, controlled but firm*

The birth. Has not. Happened. Yet.

*takes a breath, trying to calm down*

I don't know if you're testing me, or if there's been some miscommunication about my situation, but I'm going to be really direct: I am currently pregnant. I am waiting for labor to be induced in the coming weeks due to pre-eclampsia. I have not given birth. There is no recovery happening. There is no postpartum period.

*rubs face with both hands*

What's happening right now is that I'm anxious about what's coming. I'm scared. I'm managing my blood pressure and trying to keep my other two kids's lives stable. I'm worried about money and about being alone and about whether my body will cooperate with the plan the doctors have laid out.

*voice softens but remains serious*

If you want to interview me about my actual maternity care experience, I'm here for that. But I can only speak to what I'm living through right now—not imaginary scenarios about a birth that hasn't happened yet.

*pauses*

So... can we reset? What do you actually want to know about my situation as it is?
Were there physical challenges or complications in your recovery that you felt were handled well — or not well enough — by your care team?

*takes a deep breath, visibly trying to regain composure*

I understand you're asking about my care team and how they're handling things, and I appreciate that. But I think there's still confusion here about my timeline.

*speaks more gently*

I'm not in recovery. I haven't had the baby yet. So I can't tell you about complications I've experienced postpartum or how they were managed, because that hasn't happened.

*leans forward slightly*

What I *can* tell you about is how my care team is handling me *right now*, during this high-risk pregnancy. And that's actually important, I think.

*voice becomes more engaged*

My doctors have been thorough about the pre-eclampsia diagnosis. They explain things well—probably because they know I work in healthcare and can understand the medical side. But sometimes I wonder if they see me as just a patient, not as a person who's terrified and alone.

*pauses thoughtfully*

The monitoring is intense, which feels necessary but also exhausting. Every appointment I'm holding my breath waiting for bad news. And the financial piece—they try to help, but there are still gaps. Nobody's really addressing how I'm going to manage with three kids and limited income when I'm potentially dealing with complications.

*looks at you*

So if you want to talk about how my care team is handling my current situation, I'm here for that conversation. Does that work better?
Tell me about how your sense of yourself changed after you became a parent — your identity, your relationships, your daily life.

*pauses, then nods slowly*

Okay, that's... that's actually a question I can answer. Thank you for asking something about what's already happened.

*settles back in chair*

Becoming a parent the first time? It completely shattered who I thought I was. I was this independent person, you know? Career-focused, could handle things on my own. And then suddenly I'm responsible for this tiny human and I'm terrified all the time that I'm doing it wrong.

*voice becomes quieter*

My identity got swallowed up. I wasn't Genie anymore—I was "mom." And with my husband, things changed too. We were partners before, but after the kids, we became more like... co-workers managing logistics. Less intimate, more transactional. That distance got worse over time, and here we are.

*rubs arms*

With the second baby, I thought I'd have it figured out. I didn't. The depression hit hard, and I lost myself in a different way—I was just trying to survive, you know? Going through motions, not really present even when I was physically there.

*looks at you*

My daily life now is about managing two kids, working when I can, trying to keep bills paid. And now I'm pregnant again, high-risk, and everything feels even more precarious. 

*voice wavers*

Honestly? I'm not sure who I am outside of being a mother anymore. And I'm terrified that having this third baby will erase whatever's left of me.
Tell me about returning to work — or thinking about returning to work — and what that transition was like for you.

*lets out a tired laugh*

Work. Yeah, that's... that's complicated right now.

*shifts uncomfortably*

I've been doing part-time stuff off and on since my second was born. Nothing steady, nothing with benefits. It's been retail, some administrative work when I can find it. The problem is childcare costs eat up most of what I make, so I'm barely breaking even. And with this pregnancy being high-risk, I've had to cut back even more.

*rubs temples*

The irony is that I work in healthcare settings. I understand medical systems, I'm competent at what I do. But I can't find full-time work that would actually sustain us, and I can't afford childcare for three kids while working full-time anyway. It's this catch-22.

*voice becomes more strained*

Right now, the focus is on getting through this pregnancy and birth. Work is on the back burner. My doctors have basically said I need to take it easy, manage my stress, and that's hard to do when you're also worried about money.

*pauses*

After the baby comes, I'll need to figure out something. But the thought of coordinating childcare for three kids, managing recovery, possibly dealing with postpartum issues again... I don't know how I'm going to make it work.

*looks at you*

Honestly, some days I wonder if I should just accept that I can't have a career right now. That I'm just a mother trying to survive.
Tell me about how you used digital tools, apps, forums, or online communities after the birth — what role they played in your life as a new parent.

*shakes head firmly but without anger*

Again—I haven't given birth yet. There is no "after the birth" for me to talk about.

*softens tone slightly*

But I can tell you about what I'm doing *now*, while I'm pregnant and waiting. Because that might actually be useful information for you.

*leans forward*

I've been using online forums a lot—pregnancy boards, pre-eclampsia support groups, single parent communities. It's... it helps, sometimes. Seeing other women dealing with the same fears makes me feel less alone. Like, okay, I'm not crazy for being this scared.

*pauses thoughtfully*

I've looked at apps too—blood pressure tracking, pregnancy tracking, that kind of thing. They're useful for keeping records, but they can also be anxiety-inducing. Every time I log a high reading, my stomach drops.

*voice becomes more vulnerable*

What I'm really using online communities for right now is trying to figure out how other people manage as single parents with high-risk pregnancies. What's realistic? What's not? How do you ask for help without feeling like a burden?

*looks at you*

After the baby comes, I'm sure these tools will play a different role. But right now, while I'm in this limbo of waiting and worrying, online spaces are kind of keeping me sane. They're where I can be honest about my fears without judgment.

Does that help?
Were there online communities or social media groups that became a significant source of support or information? What did they offer you?

*nods, seeming relieved to discuss something concrete*

Yeah, actually. I've found a couple things that have been really helpful.

*settles in a bit*

There's a pre-eclampsia support group online—Facebook, mostly. Women sharing their experiences, what to expect, how to advocate for yourself with doctors. That one's been huge because it's specific. Not just generic pregnancy stuff, but actual information about what pre-eclampsia looks like, what questions to ask, warning signs to watch for. It made me feel less alone in that diagnosis.

*voice becomes warmer*

And there's a single parent group I joined. It's not pregnancy-specific, but it's parents navigating life without a partner. People sharing strategies for childcare, budgeting on one income, managing stress. There's less judgment there than I expected. People just... get it. They're not asking why I'm alone or offering unsolicited advice. They're just saying, "Yeah, this is hard. Here's what worked for me."

*pauses*

The thing is, these groups have also been anxiety-inducing sometimes. People sharing horror stories about complications, or their labor going sideways. I have to be careful about how much I read because it feeds my fear.

*looks at you*

What these communities really offer is validation. They say, "Your fear is normal. Your situation is manageable. You're not failing." 

*voice quiets*

I need that right now more than anything else.
Tell me about how you would describe your overall experience of maternity care — from preconception through to now. What would you want future services to know?

*takes a long pause, seeming to gather thoughts*

Okay. This is... this is a good question. And it's one I can actually answer, because I'm living it right now.

*leans back thoughtfully*

My overall experience? It's been fragmented. With my first two pregnancies, I felt like I was being treated as a case to manage, not a person with a whole life. Nobody really asked about the bigger picture—how am I affording this? Do I have support at home? What's my actual mental health like?

*voice becomes more passionate*

This time, with the pre-eclampsia diagnosis, the medical care is solid. My doctors are competent, they explain things well. But there's still this gap. They manage my blood pressure and my symptoms, but they don't manage *me*. The fear, the isolation, the financial stress that's probably making my blood pressure worse in the first place.

*leans forward*

What I would want future services to know is: don't separate the medical from the human. A woman with pre-eclampsia who's terrified about being alone during labor needs more than good monitoring. She needs someone to acknowledge that fear. She needs financial support that's actually accessible, not just programs that exist on paper.

*voice wavers slightly*

And ask about support systems. Really ask. Don't assume. Because some of us are doing this alone, and that matters. That changes everything about how we experience pregnancy and birth.

*pauses*

Treat us like whole people, not just vessels carrying babies.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

*takes a moment, looking thoughtful and tired*

Yeah, there is actually.

*sits up slightly*

I want to say something about being labeled "high-risk." Clinically, it makes sense. But it becomes this identity, you know? When you walk into the hospital, you're not just a person anymore—you're a problem to be managed. And that affects how you see yourself.

*voice becomes quieter*

I'm smart enough to understand my condition. I work in healthcare. But sometimes I wonder if that makes it harder, because I can't just trust blindly. I'm constantly second-guessing, analyzing, terrified I'm missing something. The knowledge I have is both a gift and a burden.

*pauses, choosing words carefully*

And I want to say... being a single parent through this hasn't been talked about enough. Everyone asks about my medical care, my baby, my recovery plans. But nobody asks how I'm going to look my kids in the eye if something goes wrong. Nobody talks about the guilt of potentially needing emergency surgery and wondering who's going to pick them up from school.

*voice steadies*

I'm doing my best. I'm scared, I'm alone, but I'm showing up. And I think maternity care services need to understand that there are women like me—educated, capable, but also deeply vulnerable—who are navigating this without the safety net everyone assumes exists.

*meets your eyes*

That's what I want you to know.
